By using the variable features identified by SCTransform, we can ensure that we are focusing on the most informative genes for our analysis.
:::

## ADT matrix {.tabset}

```{r CD8 MVP ADT}
DefaultAssay(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "ADT"
VariableFeatures(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- rownames(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate[["ADT"]])
```

We take all the ADT features as variable features because we have only 13 ADT features, and we want to keep all of them for downstream analysis (clustering, annotation, etc.). All are biologically relevant and we don't want to exclude any of them.

<br>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<br>

# Scaling {.tabset}

## RNA matrix {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Scale RNA}
DefaultAssay(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "RNA"
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- ScaleData(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  assay = "RNA",
  # vars.to.regress = c("nCount_RNA", "subsets_mito_percent", "nFeature_RNA", "emulsion", "subsets_ribo_percent", "Phase"),
  features = VariableFeatures(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate))
```

::: callout-tip
We don't need to Scale data if we use SCTransform because it's already performed during the normalization step. It's still debate but run Scaling or not depends mostly on what we will see (OR NOT) later on PCA, UMAP, clustering results. If we see that the technical factors (e.g., nCount_RNA, subsets_mito_percent) are driving the separation of cells in PCA/UMAP, then we can try to regress them out during Scaling to reduce their influence on downstream analyses. Otherwise, if we see that the biological signal is strong and not confounded by technical factors, we can skip Scaling and directly run PCA/UMAP on the SCT assay.
:::

## ADT matrix {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Scale ADT}
DefaultAssay(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "ADT"
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- ScaleData(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  assay = "ADT")
```

<br>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<br>

# PCA {.tabset}

## RNA matrix {.tabset}

### Run

::: callout-tip
Run PCA on the "SCT" assay, which contains the normalized and scaled data from SCTransform.
:::

We will use the variable features identified by SCTransform for PCA, which are stored in the "SCT" assay. We can specify the number of principal components (npcs) to compute based on our dataset and downstream analysis needs. In this case, we will compute 50 PCs, but this can be adjusted based on the elbow plot and the amount of variance explained by the PCs.

```{r CD8 PCA RNA Run}
DefaultAssay(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "SCT"
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- RunPCA(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
  reduction.name = "pca",
  npcs = 50,
  weight.by.var = TRUE,
  verbose = TRUE)
```

### Visualize

```{r CD8 PCA RNA ElbowPlot}
p_pca_rna <- ElbowPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
  ndims = 50, 
  reduction = "pca") +
  ggtitle("PCA Elbow Plot - RNA assay")
p_pca_rna

dir.create(
  file.path(DIR_RESULT,
            "CD8_Tcells",
            "PCA",
            "RNA"),
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE)

ggsave(
  plot = p_pca_rna,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "PCA",
    "RNA",
    "RNA_PCA_elbowplot.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

15 PCs seems to be a good choice based on the elbow plot, but we can also look at the percentage of variance explained by each PC to make a more informed decision.

::: callout-tip
We can also test different numbers of PCs (e.g., 10, 15, 20) and evaluate their impact on downstream analyses (UMAP, clustering) to find the optimal number of PCs for our dataset.
:::

```{r CD8 PCA RNA Variance Explained}
# Percentage of variance explained by each PC
pct_var <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate[["pca"]]@stdev^2
pct_var <- pct_var / sum(pct_var) * 100 

cum_var <- cumsum(pct_var)


df_var <- data.frame(
  PC = 1:length(pct_var),
  Variance = pct_var,
  CumulativeVariance = cum_var
)

p_cumvar_rna <- ggplot(df_var, aes(x = PC)) +
  geom_bar(aes(y = Variance), stat = "identity", fill = "skyblue") +
  geom_line(aes(y = CumulativeVariance), color = "red", size = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = CumulativeVariance), color = "red", size = 2) +
  ylab("Variance Explained (%)") +
  xlab("Principal Components") +
  ggtitle("Explained variance per PC with cumulative variance") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:length(pct_var)) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1)
  )
p_cumvar_rna

ggsave(
  plot = p_cumvar_rna,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "PCA",
    "RNA",
    "RNA_Variance_explained_by_PCs.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

15 PC allow us to explained almost 75% of the variance in RNA matrix

```{r CD8 PCA RNA PC Heatmap}
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 12
#| fig-cap: "PCA Heatmap - RNA assay"
#| fig-alt: "Heatmap of the top genes contributing to the first 15 PCs, which can help us understand the biological processes driving the variation in our dataset. We can look at the top genes for each PC and see if they are associated with specific pathways or cell types, which can provide insights into the underlying biology of our CD8 T cells."
#| fig-note: "Heatmap of the top genes contributing to the first 15 PCs, which can help us understand the biological processes driving the variation in our dataset. We can look at the top genes for each PC and see if they are associated with specific pathways or cell types, which can provide insights into the underlying biology of our CD8 T cells."

DefaultAssay(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "SCT"

p_pca_rna_heatmap <- DimHeatmap(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  reduction = "pca",
  dims = 1:15,
  cells = 500,
  balanced = TRUE,
  fast = TRUE
) + ggtitle("PCA Heatmap - RNA assay")

p_pca_rna_heatmap

ggsave(
  plot = p_pca_rna_heatmap,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "PCA",
    "RNA",
    "RNA_PCA_heatmap.png"),
)
```

Heatmap of the top genes contributing to the first 15 PCs, which can help us understand the biological processes driving the variation in our dataset. We can look at the top genes for each PC and see if they are associated with specific pathways or cell types, which can provide insights into the underlying biology of our CD8 T cells.

```{r CD8 PCA DimPlot RNA emulsion}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-cap: "PCA DimPlot by emulsion - RNA assay"
#| fig-alt: "PCA DimPlot colored by emulsion"
#| fig-note: "PCA DimPlot colored by emulsion, which can help us identify potential batch effects or technical variations associated with the emulsion. If we see that cells from different emulsions cluster separately in PCA space, it may indicate that there are technical differences between the emulsions that are driving the separation of cells. This can be important to consider when interpreting downstream analyses (e.g., clustering, differential expression) and may require batch correction methods (e.g., Harmony) to mitigate the influence of this technical variation."

p_pca_rna_dimplot_emulsion <- DimPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  reduction = "pca",
  group.by = "emulsion") +
  ggtitle("PCA DimPlot - RNA assay") + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal, drop = FALSE)
p_pca_rna_dimplot_emulsion

ggsave(
  plot = p_pca_rna_dimplot_emulsion,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "PCA",
    "RNA",
    "RNA_PCA_DimPlot_by_emulsion.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```
```{r CD8 PCA DimPlot RNA emulsion wrap}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 10
#| fig-cap: "PCA DimPlot by emulsion - RNA assay"
#| fig-alt: "PCA DimPlot colored by emulsion"
#| fig-note: "PCA DimPlot colored by emulsion, which can help us identify potential batch effects or technical variations associated with the emulsion. If we see that cells from different emulsions cluster separately in PCA space, it may indicate that there are technical differences between the emulsions that are driving the separation of cells. This can be important to consider when interpreting downstream analyses (e.g., clustering, differential expression) and may require batch correction methods (e.g., Harmony) to mitigate the influence of this technical variation."
p_pca_rna_dimplot_emulsion_wrap <- DimPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  reduction = "pca",
  group.by = "emulsion") +
  ggtitle("PCA DimPlot - RNA assay") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal, drop = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~emulsion, ncol = 6) +
  NoLegend()
p_pca_rna_dimplot_emulsion_wrap

ggsave(
  plot = p_pca_rna_dimplot_emulsion_wrap,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "PCA",
    "RNA",
    "RNA_PCA_DimPlot_by_emulsion_wrap.png"),
  width = 15,
  height = 10)
```

There is some separation of cells based on emulsion in PCA space, which suggests that there may be some batch effect or technical variation associated with the emulsion. This could be due to differences in sample processing, library preparation, or sequencing between emulsions. It's important to keep this in mind when interpreting downstream analyses (e.g., clustering, differential expression) and consider applying batch correction methods (e.g., Harmony) if necessary to mitigate the influence of this technical variation.

```{r CD8 PCA DimPlot RNA Phase}
p_pca_rna_dimplot_phase <- DimPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  reduction = "pca",
  group.by = "Phase") +
  ggtitle("PCA DimPlot - RNA assay")
p_pca_rna_dimplot_phase

ggsave(
  plot = p_pca_rna_dimplot_phase,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "PCA",
    "RNA",
    "RNA_PCA_DimPlot_by_Phase.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

p_pca_rna_dimplot_phase_wrap <- DimPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  reduction = "pca",
  group.by = "Phase") +
  ggtitle("PCA DimPlot - RNA assay") + 
  facet_wrap(~Phase)
p_pca_rna_dimplot_phase_wrap

ggsave(
  plot = p_pca_rna_dimplot_phase_wrap,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "PCA",
    "RNA",
    "RNA_PCA_DimPlot_by_Phase_wrap.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

No clear separation of cells based on cell cycle phase in PCA space, which is good because it means that the biological factors (e.g., cell cycle phase) are not driving the separation of cells in PCA space. This suggests that the normalization and scaling steps have successfully mitigated the influence of these factors on downstream analyses.

## ADT matrix {.tabset}

```{r CD8 PCA ADT Run}
DefaultAssay(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "ADT"
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- RunPCA(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
  reduction.name = "apca",
  npcs = 14,
  weight.by.var = TRUE,
  verbose = TRUE)
```

```{r CD8 PCA ADT ElbowPlot}
p_pca_adt <- ElbowPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
  ndims = 50, 
  reduction = "apca") +
  ggtitle("PCA Elbow Plot - RNA assay")
p_pca_adt

dir.create(
  file.path(DIR_RESULT,
            "CD8_Tcells",
            "PCA",
            "ADT"),
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE)

ggsave(
  plot = p_pca_adt,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "PCA",
    "ADT",
    "ADT_PCA_elbowplot.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

```{r CD8 PCA ADT Variance Explained}
# Percentage of variance explained by each PC
pct_var <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate[["apca"]]@stdev^2
pct_var <- pct_var / sum(pct_var) * 100 

cum_var <- cumsum(pct_var)


df_var <- data.frame(
  PC = 1:length(pct_var),
  Variance = pct_var,
  CumulativeVariance = cum_var
)

p_cumvar_adt <- ggplot(df_var, aes(x = PC)) +
  geom_bar(aes(y = Variance), stat = "identity", fill = "skyblue") +
  geom_line(aes(y = CumulativeVariance), color = "red", size = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = CumulativeVariance), color = "red", size = 2) +
  ylab("Variance Explained (%)") +
  xlab("Principal Components") +
  ggtitle("Explained variance per PC with cumulative variance") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:length(pct_var)) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1)
  )
p_cumvar_adt

ggsave(
  plot = p_cumvar_adt,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "PCA",
    "ADT",
    "ADT_Variance_explained_by_PCs.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

We take ALL !!! The 13 PCs for downstream analyses (UMAP, clustering) because we have only 13 ADT features, and we want to keep all of them for downstream analysis (clustering, annotation, etc.). All are biologically relevant and we don't want to exclude any of them.

<br>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<br>

# Batch correction (Harmony) {.tabset}

::: callout-tip
Ideally, test different batch correction methods (e.g., Harmony, Seurat's integration, MNN) and evaluate their impact on downstream analyses (PCA, UMAP, clustering) to find the optimal method for dataset.
:::

::: callout-tip
Test LISI score also BEFORE and AFTER batch correction to quantitatively evaluate the effectiveness of the batch correction method in integrating the data while preserving biological variation.
:::

## RNA matrix {.tabset}

### Before correction

#### LISI score {.tabset}

To assess the batch effect presence, we can compute the LISI (Local Inverse Simpson's Index) score, which quantifies the degree of mixing between batches (emulsions) and conditions in the embedding space.

::: callout-note
The LISI score ranges from 1 to K, where K is the number of categories in the variable being evaluated. Higher values indicate better local mixing across categories, while values close to 1 indicate strong local separation.
:::

##### Run

```{r CD8 LISI score Run before correction}
emb <- Embeddings(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, reduction = "pca")
meta <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data

lisi_scores <- compute_lisi(
  emb,
  meta,
  c("emulsion", "source", "condition")
)

summary(lisi_scores)

```

> emulsion quantifies batch mixing across the embedding: higher values indicate better batch correction.

> source & condition quantifies condition mixing: lower values indicate better preservation of biological separation between conditions.

::: callout-tip
A low batch effect is characterized by high emulsion_LISI and low condition_LISI & source_LISI, indicating that technical batch effects are not present while biological differences between HH and MM are present.
:::

##### Visualize

```{r CD8 LISI score Viz before correction}
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$emulsion_LISI  <- lisi_scores$emulsion
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$source_LISI    <- lisi_scores$source
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$condition_LISI <- lisi_scores$condition

FeaturePlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  features = c("emulsion_LISI", "source_LISI", "condition_LISI"),
  cols = c("blue", "yellow", "red"),
  min.cutoff = "q05",
  max.cutoff = "q95"
)

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Harmony",
    "CD8_RNA_Harmony_LISI_scores_before_correction.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

```

```{r CD8 LISI score Viz 2 before correction}
ggplot(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data,
       aes(x = predicted.celltype.l2, y = condition_LISI)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 0.3) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Harmony",
    "CD8_RNA_Harmony_LISI_scores_by_celltype_before_correction.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

### Correction {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Harmony RNA Run}
DefaultAssay(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "SCT"
#batch correction with Harmony (RNA)
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- RunHarmony(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
                            group.by.vars = "emulsion",
                            reduction = "pca",
                            dims.use = 1:15,
                            plot_convergence = TRUE,
                            max.iter = 20,
                            reduction.save = "harmony",
                            assay.use = "SCT",
                            verbose = TRUE,
                            theta = 2, # theta is the diversity clustering penalty param
                            lambda = NULL, # lambda is the ridge regression penalty param
                            sigma = 0.2) # sigma is the bandwidth parameter for soft k-means clustering (default 0.1, smaller values lead to more aggressive correction)
```

This plot shows the convergence of the Harmony algorithm during batch correction. The y-axis represents the objective function value (or a similar metric) that the algorithm is trying to minimize, while the x-axis represents the iteration number. A decreasing trend in the objective function value indicates that the algorithm is successfully correcting for batch effects and converging towards a solution. If the plot shows a plateau or an increase in the objective function value, it may suggest that the algorithm is not converging properly, and you may need to adjust parameters (e.g., theta, lambda, sigma) or increase the number of iterations to achieve better batch correction.

> Theta is the diversity clustering penalty parameter, which controls the strength of batch correction. Higher values will result in stronger correction, while lower values will allow for more batch-specific variation to remain in the data. The optimal value of theta can depend on the specific dataset and the degree of batch effect present, so it may require some tuning to find the best balance between correcting for batch effects and preserving biological variation.

> Ridge regression penalty : Default lambda=1. Bigger values protect against over correction. If several covariates are specified, then lambda can also be a vector which needs to be equal length with the number of variables to be corrected. In this scenario, each covariate level group will be assigned the scalars specified by the user. If set to NULL, harmony will start lambda estimation mode to determine lambdas automatically and try to minimize overcorrection (Use with caution still in beta testing).

> Width of soft kmeans clusters : Default sigma=0.1. Sigma scales the distance from a cell to cluster centroids. Larger values of sigma result in cells assigned to more clusters. Smaller values of sigma make soft kmeans cluster approach hard clustering.

### After correction {.tabset}

#### Convergence {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Harmony RNA Convergence Plot}
p_converge_Harmony <- last_plot()
p_converge_Harmony

dir.create(
  file.path(DIR_RESULT,
            "CD8_Tcells",
            "Harmony"),
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE)

ggsave(
  plot = p_converge_Harmony,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Harmony",
    "CD8_RNA_Harmony_convergence_plot.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

Convergence plot of the Harmony algorithm, which shows how the batch correction process is progressing over iterations. The plot typically displays the objective function value (or a similar metric) on the y-axis and the iteration number on the x-axis. A decreasing trend in the objective function value indicates that the algorithm is successfully correcting for batch effects and converging towards a solution. If the plot shows a plateau or an increase in the objective function value, it may suggest that the algorithm is not converging properly, and you may need to adjust parameters (e.g., theta, lambda, sigma) or increase the number of iterations to achieve better batch correction. Here we are good.

#### ElbowPlot {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Harmony RNA ElbowPlot after correction}
p_elbow_Harmony <- ElbowPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
  ndims = 50, 
  reduction = "harmony") +
  ggtitle("PCA Elbow Plot after Harmony - RNA assay")
p_elbow_Harmony

ggsave(
  plot = p_elbow_Harmony,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Harmony",
    "CD8_RNA_Harmony_PCA_elbowplot.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

::: callout-note
We have the almost same number of PCs to take into account after Harmony correction, which is good because it means that the batch correction process has not significantly altered the structure of the data in PCA space. This suggests that the Harmony algorithm has successfully corrected for batch effects while preserving the underlying biological variation in our CD8 T cell dataset.
:::

We will take 13 PC post-harmony

#### PCA {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Harmony RNA DimPlot PCA after correction}
p_pca_harmony <- DimPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  reduction = "harmony",
  group.by = "emulsion") +
  ggtitle("PCA DimPlot after Harmony - RNA assay") +
  scale_color_manual(values = colors_55)
p_pca_harmony

ggsave(
  plot = p_pca_harmony,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Harmony",
    "CD8_RNA_Harmony_PCA_DimPlot_by_emulsion.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```
```{r CD8 Harmony RNA DimPlot PCA by emulsion wrap after correction}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 10
#| fig-cap: "PCA DimPlot by emulsion after Harmony - RNA assay"
#| fig-note: "PCA DimPlot colored by emulsion after Harmony correction, which can help us evaluate the effectiveness of the batch correction process. If we see that cells from different emulsions are now well mixed in PCA space, it suggests that the Harmony algorithm has successfully mitigated the influence of the emulsion on the separation of cells. This allows us to focus on the underlying biological variation in our CD8 T cell dataset without confounding technical effects."

p_pca_harmony_wrap <- DimPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  reduction = "harmony",
  group.by = "emulsion") +
  ggtitle("PCA DimPlot after Harmony - RNA assay") +
  facet_wrap(~emulsion, ncol = 6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = colors_55) +
  NoLegend()
p_pca_harmony_wrap

ggsave(
  plot = p_pca_harmony_wrap,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Harmony",
    "CD8_RNA_Harmony_PCA_DimPlot_by_emulsion_wrap.png"),
  width = 15,
  height = 10)
```

We cannot see any separation of cells based on emulsion in PCA space after Harmony correction, which is good because it means that the batch effect associated with the emulsion has been successfully corrected. This suggests that the Harmony algorithm has effectively mitigated the influence of the emulsion on the separation of cells in PCA space, allowing us to focus on the underlying biological variation in our CD8 T cell dataset without confounding technical effects.

#### LISI score {.tabset}

To assess the quality of our batch correction, we can compute the LISI (Local Inverse Simpson's Index) score, which quantifies the degree of mixing between batches (emulsions) and conditions in the embedding space.

::: callout-note
The LISI score ranges from 1 to K, where K is the number of categories in the variable being evaluated. Higher values indicate better local mixing across categories, while values close to 1 indicate strong local separation.
:::

##### Run

```{r CD8 LISI score Run after Harmony}
emb <- Embeddings(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, reduction = "harmony")
meta <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data

lisi_scores <- compute_lisi(
  emb,
  meta,
  c("emulsion", "source", "condition")
)

summary(lisi_scores)

```

> emulsion quantifies batch mixing across the embedding: higher values indicate better batch correction.

> source & condition quantifies condition mixing: lower values indicate better preservation of biological separation between conditions.

::: callout-tip
An optimal integration is therefore characterized by high emulsion_LISI and low condition_LISI & source_LISI, indicating that technical batch effects are removed while biological differences between HH and MM are preserved.
:::

We achieved effective correction of technical batch effects, as indicated by high emulsion LISI scores. However, we also observed increased mixing across biological variables such as condition and source. This suggests that Harmony may have partially overcorrected the data, potentially reducing biologically meaningful structure. This illustrates the inherent trade-off in batch correction between removing unwanted technical variation and preserving true biological heterogeneity, emphasizing the need for careful parameter tuning.

##### Visualize

```{r CD8 LISI score Viz after Harmony}
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$emulsion_LISI  <- lisi_scores$emulsion
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$source_LISI    <- lisi_scores$source
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$condition_LISI <- lisi_scores$condition

FeaturePlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  features = c("emulsion_LISI", "source_LISI", "condition_LISI"),
  cols = c("blue", "yellow", "red"),
  min.cutoff = "q05",
  max.cutoff = "q95"
)

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Harmony",
    "CD8_RNA_Harmony_LISI_scores.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

```

```{r CD8 LISI score Viz 2 after Harmony}
ggplot(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data,
       aes(x = predicted.celltype.l2, y = condition_LISI)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 0.3) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Harmony",
    "CD8_RNA_Harmony_LISI_scores_by_celltype.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

```{r CD8 LISI score comparison before and after Harmony}
emb_raw <- Embeddings(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, reduction = "pca")
emb_int <- Embeddings(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, reduction = "harmony")

lisi_raw  <- compute_lisi(emb_raw,  meta, c("emulsion", "condition"))
lisi_int  <- compute_lisi(emb_int,  meta, c("emulsion", "condition"))

df <- rbind(
  data.frame(score = lisi_raw$emulsion, type = "Raw", var = "Batch"),
  data.frame(score = lisi_int$emulsion, type = "Integrated", var = "Batch"),
  data.frame(score = lisi_raw$condition, type = "Raw", var = "Condition"),
  data.frame(score = lisi_int$condition, type = "Integrated", var = "Condition")
)

ggplot(df, aes(type, score, fill = type)) +
  geom_violin(trim = TRUE) +
  facet_wrap(~var, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_classic() +
  NoLegend() +
  ggtitle("LISI scores before and after Harmony correction") +
  labs(y = "LISI Score (Mixing/Integration)")

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Harmony",
    "CD8_RNA_Harmony_LISI_scores_comparison.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

We can see that the emulsion_LISI score has significantly increased after Harmony correction, indicating improved batch mixing and successful correction of batch effects. However, the condition_LISI score has also increased, suggesting that some biological separation between conditions may have been lost during the batch correction process.

<br>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<br>

# Communities detection {.tabset}

Now that we have our PCA embeddings (RNA and ADT) and our batch-corrected embeddings (Harmony), we can proceed to the community detection step, which involves clustering the cells based on their similarity in the embedding space. This step is crucial for identifying distinct cell populations and understanding the heterogeneity within our CD8 T cell dataset. We will use the FindMultiModalNeighbors function to construct a shared nearest neighbor graph that integrates both RNA and ADT information, and then apply the FindClusters function to identify clusters of cells. We will also optimize the clustering parameters (e.g., k, resolution) to find the best clustering solution for our dataset.

## Tuning {.tabset}

Workflow and loop for k and res optimisation to find the best clustering solution for our CD8 T cell dataset. We will run this in parallel to speed up the process, and we will save the results (e.g., silhouette scores, cluster assignments) for each combination of parameters to evaluate their performance later on. This will allow us to systematically explore the parameter space and identify the optimal settings for our clustering analysis.

::: callout-note
IT IS VERY LONG !!! (15-20mins) TO RUN ALL THE COMBINATIONS OF PARAMETERS, SO IT'S COMMENTED
:::

```{r CD8 Clusters stability optimization workflow}

# 
# plan(multicore, workers = parallel::detectCores() - 1)
# 
# run_and_save_parallel <- function(params, seu, dims_use, base_dir) {
#   
#   k <- params$k
#   resolution <- params$resolution
#   
#   message("Running k=", k, " res=", resolution)
#   
#   seu_tmp <- seu
#   
#   # seu_tmp <- FindNeighbors(
#   #   seu_tmp,
#   #   reduction = "harmony",
#   #   dims = dims_use,
#   #   k.param = k,
#   #   verbose = FALSE
#   # )
#   
#   seu_tmp <- FindMultiModalNeighbors(seu_tmp,
#                                reduction.list = list("harmony", "apca"),
#                                dims.list = dims_list,
#                                k.nn = k,
#                                verbose = TRUE)
#   
#   seu_tmp <- FindClusters(
#     seu_tmp,
#     resolution = resolution,
#     graph.name = "wsnn",
#     verbose = FALSE
#   )
#   
#   seu_tmp <- RunUMAP(
#     seu_tmp,
#     reduction = "harmony",
#     dims = dims_use,
#     verbose = FALSE
#   )
#   
#   clust <- as.integer(Idents(seu_tmp))
#   emb <- Embeddings(seu_tmp, "harmony")[, dims_use]
#   
#   sil <- silhouette(clust, dist(emb))
#   
#   sil_df <- data.frame(
#     sil = sil[, "sil_width"],
#     cluster = factor(clust)
#   )
#   
#   mean_sil <- mean(sil_df$sil)
#   prop_neg <- mean(sil_df$sil < 0)
#   n_clusters <- length(unique(clust))
#   
#   # Choisir palette adaptée
#   fill_colors <- if (n_clusters <= length(pal)) {
#     pal[1:n_clusters]
#   } else {
#     colors_55[1:n_clusters]
#   }
#   
#   param_name <- paste0("k", k,
#                        "_res", resolution,
#                        "_dims", min(dims_use), "-", max(dims_use))
#   
#   param_dir <- file.path(base_dir, param_name)
#   dir.create(param_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)
#   
#   # # --- Clust tree ---
#   # clustree(seu_tmp, prefix = "wsnn_res.")
#   # 
#   # dir.create(
#   #   file.path(DIR_RESULT,
#   #             "CD8_Tcells",
#   #             "Clustering"),
#   #   recursive = TRUE,
#   #   showWarnings = FALSE)
#   # 
#   # ggsave(
#   #   filename = file.path(
#   #     param_dir,
#   #     "Clustree_WNN.png"),
#   #   width = 12,
#   #   height = 12)
#   
#   # ---- Plot violin ----
#   p1 <- ggplot(sil_df,
#                aes(x = cluster, y = sil, fill = cluster)) +
#     geom_violin(trim = FALSE) +
#     geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, fill = "white") +
#     geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
#     theme_minimal() +
#     labs(title = paste0("k=", k,
#                         " res=", resolution,
#                         "\nmean_sil=", round(mean_sil, 3),
#                         " neg=", round(prop_neg, 3)),
#          x = "Cluster", 
#          y = "Silhouette width") +
#   scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors)
#   
#   ggsave(file.path(param_dir, "silhouette_violin.png"),
#          p1, width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300)
#   
#   # ---- Histogram ----
#   all_silouhette <- 
#     ggplot(sil_df, aes(x = sil, fill = cluster)) +
#       geom_histogram(bins = 100, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
#       geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
#       theme_minimal() +
#       labs(x = "Silhouette width", y = "Cell count", fill = "Cluster") + 
#       scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors)
#   all_silouhette
#   
#   ggsave(
#     plot = all_silouhette,
#     file.path(
#       param_dir, 
#       "silhouette_hist.png"),
#     width = 8, 
#     height = 6, dpi = 300)
#   
#   # --- Plot mean silhouette per cluster ----
#   mean_sil <- sil_df %>%
#   group_by(cluster) %>%
#   summarise(mean_sil = mean(sil))
# 
#   mean_silouhette <- ggplot(mean_sil, aes(x = cluster, y = mean_sil, fill = cluster)) +
#     geom_col() +
#     geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
#     theme_minimal() +
#     labs(x = "Cluster", y = "Mean silhouette width") +
#     scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors)
#   mean_silouhette
#   
#   ggsave(
#     plot = mean_silouhette,
#     filename =
#       file.path(
#         param_dir,
#         "Silhouette_mean_width_per_cluster.png"),
#     width = 12,
#     height = 6)
#   
#   # --- Plot silhouette classic ----
#   png(filename = file.path(
#       param_dir,
#       "Silhouette_classic_plot.png"),
#       width = 2000, 
#       height = 1500, 
#       res = 300)
# 
#   classic_silouhette <- plot(sil, border = NA)
#   classic_silouhette
#   
#   dev.off()
#   
#   # --- UMAP colored by silhouette width ----
#   seu_tmp$silhouette <- sil[, "sil_width"]
#   p_umap_silhouette <- FeaturePlot(
#     seu_tmp,
#     features = "silhouette",
#     cols = c("blue", "white", "red"),
#     order = TRUE
#   ) + ggtitle(paste0("k=", k, " res=", resolution))
#   
#   ggsave(
#     plot = p_umap_silhouette,
#     filename = file.path(
#       param_dir,
#       "UMAP_silhouette.png"),
#     width = 12,
#     height = 6)
#   
#   # --- UMAP Density colored by cluster ---- 
#   df <- as.data.frame(
#     Embeddings(seu_tmp, "umap")
#   )
#   
#   colnames(df) <- c("UMAP_1", "UMAP_2")
#   
#   # Ajouter metadata utile
#   df$condition    <- seu_tmp$condition
#   # df$BCL6_status  <- seu_tmp$BCL6_status
#   df$seurat_cluster <- seu_tmp$seurat_clusters
#   
#   
#   p_umap_density_clusters <- ggplot(df, aes(UMAP_1, UMAP_2)) +
#     # Points colorés par cluster
#     geom_point(
#       aes(color = seurat_cluster),
#       size = 0.5,
#       alpha = 0.6
#     ) +
#     scale_color_manual(values = fill_colors) +
#     
#     # Contours en arrière-plan (structure globale)
#     stat_density_2d(
#       bins = 14,
#       contour_var = "density",
#       color = "black",
#       linewidth = 0.2,
#       h = c(0.8, 0.8)
#     ) +
#     
#     coord_equal() +
#     labs(title = "UMAP density plot of CD8 T cells",
#          x = "UMAP 1",
#          y = "UMAP 2",
#          color = "Cluster") +
#     
#     theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
#     theme(
#       axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
#       axis.text.y = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
#       legend.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),    # taille + gras du titre
#       legend.text  = element_text(size = 12)
#     ) +
#     
#     # Agrandir les points dans la légende
#     guides(color = guide_legend(
#       override.aes = list(size = 4)  # taille des symboles dans la légende
#     ))
#   p_umap_density_clusters
#   
#   ggsave(
#     plot = p_umap_density_clusters,
#     filename = file.path(
#       param_dir,
#       "UMAP_density_clusters.png"),
#     width = 12,
#     height = 6)
#   
#   
#   
#   
#   # --- Return results ----
#   
#   return(data.frame(
#     k = k,
#     resolution = resolution,
#     n_clusters = n_clusters,
#     mean_sil = mean_sil,
#     prop_negative = prop_neg
#   ))
# }
# 
# dims_list <- list(1:13, 1:13)
# dims_use <- 1:13
# 
# k_values <- c(20, 30, 40)
# res_values <- seq(0.2, 1.0, by = 0.2)
# 
# grid <- expand.grid(k = k_values,
#                     resolution = res_values)
# 
# base_dir <- file.path(DIR_RESULT, "CD8_Tcells", "Clustering", "Tuning_k_res")
# 
# dir.create(
#   file.path(base_dir),
#   recursive = TRUE,
#   showWarnings = FALSE)
# 
# all_results <- future_map_dfr(
#   split(grid, seq(nrow(grid))),
#   run_and_save_parallel,
#   seu = seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
#   dims_use = dims_use,
#   base_dir = base_dir,
#   .options = furrr_options(seed = TRUE)
# )

```

## Final choice {.tabset}

### Weighted Nearest Neighbor (WNN) graph {.tabset}

::: callout-note
It is really hard to find good and trustable communities in this dataset, as the silhouette scores are quite low for all the combinations of parameters we tested. This suggests that the clusters identified by the FindMultiModalNeighbors and FindClusters functions may not be well-defined or stable.
:::

According to the tuning results, we will choose k = 20 and resolution = 0.2 for our final clustering solution, as it provides a good balance between cluster stability (mean silhouette width) and biological relevance (number of clusters). We will then run the FindMultiModalNeighbors function with these parameters to construct the WNN graph that integrates both RNA and ADT information for our CD8 T cell dataset.

```{r CD8 WNN}
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- FindMultiModalNeighbors(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
                               reduction.list = list("harmony", "apca"),
                               dims.list = list(1:13, 1:13),
                               k.nn = 20,
                               verbose = TRUE)
```

### UMAP {.tabset}

#### Tuning {.tabset}

Best parameters seems to be (hard to tell, no big diff) : min.dist = 0.09 spread = 1.5 n.neighbors = 40

::: callout-note
I have commented the tuning loop for UMAP because it takes a lot of time to run all the combinations of parameters, but we can uncomment it and run it if we want to explore different UMAP parameters and visualize the results.
:::

```{r CD8 Tuning UMAP}
# # Dir  to save
# dir.create(
#   file.path(DIR_RESULT,
#             "CD8_Tcells",
#             "UMAP",
#             "Tuning"),
#   recursive = TRUE,
#   showWarnings = FALSE)
# 
# params <- expand.grid(
#   n.neighbors = c(40),
#   min.dist    = c(0.04, 0.05, 0.06, 0.07, 0.08, 0.09),
#   spread      = c(1.5, 2)
# )
# 
# for (i in seq_len(nrow(params))) {
# 
#   md <- params$min.dist[i]
#   sp <- params$spread[i]
#   nn <- params$n.neighbors[i]
# 
#   red_name <- paste0("umap_md", md, "_sp", sp, "_nn", nn)
# 
#   seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- RunUMAP(
#     seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
#     nn.name     = "weighted.nn",
#     min.dist    = md,
#     spread      = sp,
#     n.neighbors = nn,
#     seed.use    = 42,
#     reduction.name = red_name
#   )
# 
#   p <- DimPlot(
#     seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
#     reduction = red_name,
#     group.by  = "predicted.celltype.l2") +
#     ggtitle(paste("min.dist =", md,
#                   "spread =", sp,
#                   "n.neighbors =", nn)) +
#     scale_color_manual(values = pal)
# 
#   ggsave(
#     plot = p,
#     filename = file.path(
#       DIR_RESULT,
#       "CD8_Tcells",
#       "UMAP",
#       "Tuning",
#       paste0(red_name, ".png")
#     ),
#     width = 12,
#     height = 6
#   )
# }

```

#### Run {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Run UMAP}
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- RunUMAP(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  nn.name = "weighted.nn",
  min.dist = 0.09,
  spread = 1.5,
  n.neighbors = 40,
  seed.use = 42)
```

#### Visualization {.tabset}

::: callout-tip
Add density lines on UMAP to better visualize the distribution of cells in UMAP space and identify potential clusters or subpopulations. Density lines can help highlight areas of high cell density, which may correspond to distinct cell types or states. This can be particularly useful when visualizing complex datasets with many cells, as it can provide additional insights into the structure of the data and help guide downstream analyses (e.g., clustering, differential expression).
:::

::: callout-note
In the next visualization, we took the previous automatic annotations performed by PPA to have an oulook on the distribution of the different predicted cell types in UMAP space, and see if they are well separated or not.
:::

```{r CD8 UMAP Viz 1}
# Visualization
metadata <- c("condition", 
              "source", 
              "id", 
              "predicted.celltype.l1", 
              "predicted.celltype.l2", 
              "predicted.celltype.l3")

dir.create(
  file.path(DIR_RESULT,
            "CD8_Tcells",
            "UMAP",
            "Metadata"),
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE)
  
for (meta in metadata) {
  p1 <- DimPlot(
    seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
    reduction = "umap", 
    group.by = meta) + 
    ggtitle(meta) +
    scale_color_manual(values = colors_55)
  print(p)
  
  ggsave(
    plot = p1,
    filename = file.path(
      DIR_RESULT,
      "CD8_Tcells",
      "UMAP",
      "Metadata",
      paste0("UMAP_metadata_", meta, ".png")),
    width = 12,
    height = 6)
  
  p2 <- DimPlot(
    seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
    reduction = "umap", 
    group.by = meta,
    split.by = meta) + 
    ggtitle(meta)
  print(p)
  
  ggsave(
    plot = p2,
    filename = file.path(
      DIR_RESULT,
      "CD8_Tcells",
      "UMAP",
      "Metadata",
      paste0("UMAP_metadata_split", meta, ".png")),
    width = 12,
    height = 6)
}
```

I don't see a big deifference in the distrivution of cells according MM and HD, or between Bonne marrow (BMMC) and blood (PBMC). Interestingly, we can also see that we have conflict between my CD8 T cells and predicted CD4 T cells from PPA because in this dataset of CD8 T cells, we can see that some cells are annotated as CD4 T cells by PPA. Theorically, we should not have any CD4 T cells in this dataset, as we have only selected CD8 T cells based on the expression of CD8A with CITE-seq data.

::: callout-note
That's why we must be careful when using automatic annotations, as they can sometimes be inaccurate or inconsistent with the actual cell types present in the dataset.
:::

```{r CD8 UMAP Viz 3}
df_umap <- as.data.frame(Embeddings(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, "umap")) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(id = seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data$id,
                celltype = seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data$predicted.celltype.l2,
                condition = seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data$condition)


ggplot(df_umap, aes(x = umap_1, y = umap_2, color = condition)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ id, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "UMAP per patient (id)", color = "Condition") +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8))

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "UMAP",
    "Metadata",
    "UMAP_by_id.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

We can see the distribution of cells in UMAP space for each patient (id) and see if there are any patient-specific clusters or patterns.

::: callout-tip
This can help us identify potential batch effects or patient-specific differences that may need to be accounted for in downstream analyses (e.g., differential expression, clustering). It can also provide insights into the heterogeneity of CD8 T cells across different patients and conditions
:::

### Clustering {.tabset}

We will now proceed to the clustering step, which involves identifying distinct cell populations based on their similarity in the embedding space. We will use the FindClusters function to perform community detection on the WNN graph that we constructed earlier, and we will optimize the clustering resolution parameter to find the best clustering solution for our CD8 T cell dataset. We will also visualize the clusters on UMAP to assess their separation and biological relevance.

::: callout-tip
Maybe try also to cluster with SCE to see if we can get better clusters.
:::

::: callout-tip
Maybe try also other algorithms (e.g., Leiden) to see if we can get better clusters
:::

#### Test several clustering resolutions {.tabset}

We construct a tree of clusters obtained with different resolutions to visualize the stability of clusters across resolutions and identify potential hierarchical relationships between clusters.

::: callout-note
This can help us choose an optimal resolution for our clustering analysis, as well as provide insights into the structure of the data and the relationships between different cell populations in our CD8 T cell dataset.
:::

```{r CD8 FindClusters}
# # Clustering (commun RNA + ADT on WNN graph)
# Explore different resolutions

# remove previous clustering results if exist


resolutions <- seq(0.01, 0.5, by = 0.01)
for (res in resolutions) {
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- FindClusters(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, graph.name = "wsnn", resolution = res, verbose = FALSE)
}
```

#### Visualization {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Viz clusters (Clustree)}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 10
#| fig-cap: "Clustree of WNN clusters across different resolutions"
#| fig-note: "Clustree visualization of clusters obtained with different resolutions, which can help us assess the stability of clusters across resolutions and identify potential hierarchical relationships between clusters. This can guide us in choosing an optimal resolution for our clustering analysis and provide insights into the structure of the data and the relationships between different cell populations in our CD8 T cell dataset."

clustree(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, prefix = "wsnn_res.")

# Save the plot
dir.create(
  file.path(DIR_RESULT,
            "CD8_Tcells",
            "Clustering"),
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE)

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Clustree_WNN.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 12)
```

#### Optimal resolution {.tabset}

We will choose resolution 0.1 for downstream analysis

::: callout-tip
Sur-clustering is sometimes better for annotatio as we can always merge clusters later on if needed.
:::

```{r CD8 Choose opti res}
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- FindClusters(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, graph.name = "wsnn", resolution = 0.1, verbose = FALSE)

# Choose resolution 0.06
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$wsnn_res.0.1

# Reorganize clusters names
seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters <- factor(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters, 
                              levels = names(sort(table(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters), 
                                                  decreasing = TRUE)))
```

==\> We have found 8 clusters !

#### Clusters on UMAP {.tabset}

SO, the algo found 8 clusters in our CD8 T cell dataset. We will need to check the quality of those and the distribution of these clusters across conditions (MM vs HD) and sources (BMMC vs PBMC) to see if they are biologically relevant and if they correspond to known CD8 T cell subpopulations. We can also look at the expression of marker genes in each cluster to further characterize their identity and function.

```{r CD8 Clusters on UMAP}
DimPlot(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, reduction = "umap", group.by = "seurat_clusters") +
  scale_color_manual(values = pal) +
  ggtitle("seurat_clusters")

# Save the plot
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "UMAP_seurat_clusters.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "UMAP",
    "Metadata",
    "UMAP_seurat_clusters.pdf"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

DimPlot(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, reduction = "umap", group.by = "seurat_clusters") +
  scale_color_manual(values = pal) +
  ggtitle("seurat clusters") +
  facet_wrap(~seurat_clusters) +
  NoLegend()

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "UMAP_seurat_clusters_facet.png"),
  width = 15,
  height = 10)

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "UMAP",
    "Metadata",
    "UMAP_seurat_clusters_facet.pdf"),
  width = 15,
  height = 10)
```

##### UMAP density per cluster {.tabset}

We can represent these clusters on UMAP with density lines to better visualize the distribution of cells in UMAP space and identify potential clusters or subpopulations.

```{r CD8 UMAP density per cluster}
### UMAP density

df <- as.data.frame(
  Embeddings(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, "umap")
)

colnames(df) <- c("UMAP_1", "UMAP_2")

# Ajouter metadata utile
df$condition    <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$condition
# df$BCL6_status  <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$BCL6_status
df$seurat_cluster <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters


p_umap_density_clusters <- ggplot(df, aes(UMAP_1, UMAP_2)) +
  # Points colorés par cluster
  geom_point(
    aes(color = seurat_cluster),
    size = 0.5,
    alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal) +
  
  # Contours en arrière-plan (structure globale)
  stat_density_2d(
    bins = 14,
    contour_var = "density",
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.2,
    h = c(0.8, 0.8)
  ) +
  
  coord_equal() +
  labs(title = "UMAP density plot of CD8 T cells",
       x = "UMAP 1",
       y = "UMAP 2",
       color = "Cluster") +
  
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),    # taille + gras du titre
    legend.text  = element_text(size = 12)
  ) +
  
  # Agrandir les points dans la légende
  guides(color = guide_legend(
    override.aes = list(size = 4)  # taille des symboles dans la légende
  ))
p_umap_density_clusters

ggsave(
  plot = p_umap_density_clusters,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "UMAP_seurat_clusters_density.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

ggsave(
  plot = p_umap_density_clusters,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "UMAP",
    "Metadata",
    "UMAP_seurat_clusters_density.pdf"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

#### Clusters quality {.tabset}

Let's see if clusters found are not driven by technical artifacts by looking at the distribution of QC metrics (e.g., number of genes, number of UMIs, percentage of mitochondrial genes) across clusters.

::: callout-note
If we see that some clusters have significantly higher or lower QC metrics than others, it may indicate that these clusters are driven by technical artifacts rather than biological differences. We can also look at the distribution of cells across clusters to see if there are any clusters that are dominated by a single patient or condition, which may also indicate potential batch effects or technical artifacts. Finally, we can look at the expression of known marker genes in each cluster to further assess their biological relevance and identify potential cell types or states represented by each cluster.
:::

##### QC metrics per cluster {.tabset}

```{r CD8 QC metrics per clusters}
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of QC metrics across clusters"
#| fig-note: "Violin plots showing the distribution of QC metrics (number of genes, number of UMIs, percentage of mitochondrial genes) across clusters. This can help us assess the quality of clusters and identify potential technical artifacts that may be driving the clustering results. If we see that some clusters have significantly higher or lower QC metrics than others, it may indicate that these clusters are driven by technical artifacts rather than biological differences. We can also look at the distribution of cells across clusters to see if there are any clusters that are dominated by a single patient or condition, which may also indicate potential batch effects or technical artifacts. Finally, we can look at the expression of known marker genes in each cluster to further assess their biological relevance and identify potential cell types or states represented by each cluster."

VlnPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  features = c("nFeature_RNA", "nCount_RNA", "percent.mt"),
  cols = pal,
  group.by = "seurat_clusters",
  pt.size = 0,
  ncol = 3)

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Clusters_QC_metrics.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

No clear technical artifacts seem to be driving the clusters, as the distribution of QC metrics (number of genes, number of UMIs, percentage of mitochondrial genes) appears to be relatively similar across clusters.

##### Cell counts per cluster {.tabset}

Cluster 0 have more cells than the others, and cluster 7 is the smallest one.

```{r CD8 Cell counts per clusters}
# Plot number of cells per cluster with percentage labels of total cells
cell_counts <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data %>%
  count(seurat_clusters, name = "Cell_Count") %>%
  mutate(
    Percentage = round(100 * Cell_Count / sum(Cell_Count), 6),
    label = paste0(Percentage, "%")
  )


ggplot(cell_counts, aes(y = seurat_clusters, x = Cell_Count, fill = seurat_clusters)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), hjust = -0.1, size = 3) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Number (and %) of cells per cluster",
    x = "Number of cells",
    y = "Cluster"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal)

# Save the plot
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Clusters_cell_counts.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

##### Cluster repartition per patients {.tabset}

We look at the distribution of cells across clusters for each patient to see if there are any clusters that are dominated by a single patient, which may indicate potential batch effects or technical artifacts.

::: callout-note
If we see that some clusters have a disproportionate number of cells from a single patient, it may suggest that these clusters are driven by technical artifacts rather than biological differences.
:::

```{r CD8 Clusters repartition per patients}
# Proportion of cells per cluster for each patient
cluster_patient <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data %>%
  group_by(id, seurat_clusters) %>%
  summarise(Cell_Count = n()) %>%
  group_by(id) %>%
  mutate(Percentage = Cell_Count / sum(Cell_Count) * 100)

ggplot(cluster_patient, aes(x = id, y = Percentage, fill = seurat_clusters)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Proportion of cells per cluster for each patient",
    x = "Patient ID",
    y = "Proportion of cells",
    fill = "Cluster"
  ) + 
  theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, 
                                 hjust = 1, 
                                 vjust = 1)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal)

# Save the plot
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Clusters_repartition_per_patient.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

There are some clusters that are enriched in specific patients, which could be due to biological differences between patients (e.g., different disease states, immune responses) or technical variation (e.g., batch effects). Often those patients-specific clusters are the ones with the smallest number of cells, which can make them more susceptible to technical artifacts. For example cluster 6.

::: callout-note
It's important to investigate these clusters further to determine if they represent biologically meaningful subpopulations of CD8 T cells or if they are artifacts of the data. This can be done by looking at the expression of marker genes, performing differential expression analysis, and considering the clinical metadata associated with each patient.
:::

##### Mitochondrial content per cluster {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Mito per clusters}
stats_mt <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data %>%
  group_by(seurat_clusters) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_pct_mt   = mean(percent.mt),
    median_pct_mt = median(percent.mt),
    sd_pct_mt     = sd(percent.mt)
  )

kable(stats_mt, format = "html") %>%
  kable_styling()

# Save the table
write.table(stats_mt, 
            file = file.path(
              DIR_RESULT,
              "CD8_Tcells",
              "Clustering",
              "cluster_mitochondrial_content_stats.tsv"), 
            row.names = FALSE, 
            sep = "\t")
```

All clusters have a similar mitochondrial content, with mean percentage of mitochondrial genes around 4-7%, which is within the expected range for healthy cells. This suggests that the clusters identified in our CD8 T cell dataset are not driven by differences in mitochondrial content, and that they likely represent biologically meaningful subpopulations of CD8 T cells rather than technical artifacts.

```{r CD8 Viz Mito per clusters}
ggplot(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data,
       aes(
         x = percent.mt,
         y = seurat_clusters,
         fill = seurat_clusters
       )) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    alpha = 0.7,
    scale = 1.2,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    color = "white") +
  # theme_ridges() +
  # theme(
  #   legend.position = "none"
  # ) +
  # geom_vline(xintercept = 20, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(
    x = "% mitochondrial genes",
    y = "Clusters") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal)

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Clusters_mitochondrial_content_ridges.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

##### Cell cycle phase per cluster {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Cell cycle phase per clusters}
df <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate@meta.data %>%
  group_by(seurat_clusters, Phase) %>%
  summarise(n = n()) %>%
  mutate(freq = n / sum(n))

ggplot(df, aes(x = seurat_clusters, y = freq, fill = Phase)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  labs(x = "Cluster", y = "Proportion of cells", fill = "Cell cycle phase") +
  theme_minimal() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of cell cycle phases per cluster") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Clusters_cell_cycle_phase_distribution.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

ggplot(df, aes(x = seurat_clusters, y = n, fill = Phase)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  labs(x = "Cluster", y = "Number of cells", fill = "Cell cycle phase") +
  theme_minimal() +
  ggtitle("Cell counts per cell cycle phase and cluster") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Clusters_cell_cycle_phase_counts.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

mat <- df %>%
  select(seurat_clusters, Phase, freq) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Phase, values_from = freq, values_fill = 0) %>%
  column_to_rownames("seurat_clusters")

pheatmap(as.matrix(mat),
         cluster_rows = TRUE,
         cluster_cols = FALSE,
         display_numbers = TRUE,
         main = "Cell cycle phase proportions per cluster")

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Clusters_cell_cycle_phase_heatmap.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

DimPlot(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
  reduction = "umap",
  group.by = "seurat_clusters",
  split.by = "Phase",
  cols = pal
) + ggtitle("UMAP colored by cell cycle phase")

ggsave(
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "UMAP_seurat_clusters_split_by_cell_cycle_phase.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

Absolut no correslation between clusters and cell cycle phase, which suggests that the clusters identified in our CD8 T cell dataset are not driven by differences in cell cycle phase, and that they likely represent biologically meaningful subpopulations of CD8 T cells rather than technical artifacts.

##### Stability

::: callout-note
It work but too long to run
:::

```{r CD8 Clusters stability}
# library(fossil)
# 
# compute_stability <- function(seu, resolution, k, n_iter = 20) {
#   
#   ari_vec <- numeric(n_iter)
#   
#   for (i in 1:n_iter) {
#     
#     print(paste("Iteration", i, "of", n_iter))
#     
#     cells_sub <- sample(colnames(seu), 
#                         size = 0.8 * ncol(seu))
#     
#     seu_sub <- subset(seu, cells = cells_sub)
#     
#     print(paste("Subsampled dataset with", ncol(seu_sub), "cells"))
#     
#     print("Finding multi-modal neighbors...")
#     seu_sub <- FindMultiModalNeighbors(
#       seu_sub,
#       reduction.list = list("harmony", "apca"),
#       dims.list = list(1:13, 1:13),
#       k.nn = k,
#       verbose = FALSE
#     )
#     
#     print("Finding clusters...")
#     seu_sub <- FindClusters(
#       seu_sub,
#       graph.name = "wsnn",
#       resolution = resolution,
#       verbose = FALSE
#     )
#     
#     print("Computing ARI...")
#     ari_vec[i] <- adj.rand.index(
#       seu$seurat_clusters[cells_sub],
#       seu_sub$seurat_clusters
#     )
#   }
#   
#   mean(ari_vec)
# }
# 
# 
# resolutions <- c(0.05, 0.1, 0.2)
# ks <- c(10, 20, 30)
# 
# results <- expand.grid(resolution = resolutions,
#                        k = ks)
# 
# results$ARI <- NA
# 
# for (i in 1:nrow(results)) {
#   print(paste("Testing resolution =", results$resolution[i], "and k =", results$k[i]))
#   results$ARI[i] <- compute_stability(
#     seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
#     resolution = results$resolution[i],
#     k = results$k[i]
#   )
# }
# 
# 
# 
# stability_tuning <- ggplot(results, aes(x = factor(k), y = ARI, fill = factor(resolution))) +
#   geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
#   theme_bw()
# stability_tuning
# 
# 
# ggsave(
#   plot = stability_tuning,
#   filename = file.path(
#     DIR_RESULT,
#     "CD8_Tcells",
#     "Clustering",
#     "Clusters_stability_comparison.png"),
#   width = 12,
#   height = 6)
```

::: callout-note
Dont't work but maybe interesting to try if I have time, it is a more robust way to assess cluster stability by using the bootstrapStability function from the bluster package, which provides a probabilistic measure of cluster stability based on resampling the data and re-clustering multiple times. This approach can help identify clusters that are consistently found across different subsamples of the data, providing a more reliable assessment of cluster stability compared to traditional methods like silhouette scores or ARI.
:::

```{r boostrapCLuster, error=TRUE}
# sce <- as.SingleCellExperiment(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate)
# reducedDim(sce, "WNN") <- Embeddings(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, "wnn")
# 
# # ass_prob <- bluster::bootstrapStability(sce, FUN = function(x) {
# #     g <- buildSNNGraph(x, use.dimred = 'PCA_sub')
# #     igraph::cluster_walktrap(g)$membership
# #   },
# #   clusters = sce$seurat_clusters
# # )
# library(BiocParallel)
# # Choix du backend
# 
# param <- MulticoreParam(workers = 25)
# register(param)
# 
# ass_prob <- bluster::bootstrapStability(
#   sce,
#   FUN = function(x) {
#     g <- buildSNNGraph(x, use.dimred = "WNN", k = 20)
#     igraph::cluster_leiden(g)$membership
#   },
#   clusters = sce$seurat_clusters
# )
# 
# p <- ass_prob %>%
#   as_tibble() %>%
#   rownames_to_column(var = 'cluster_1') %>%
#   pivot_longer(
#     cols = 2:ncol(.),
#     names_to = 'cluster_2',
#     values_to = 'probability'
#   ) %>%
#   mutate(
#     cluster_1 = as.character(as.numeric(cluster_1) - 1),
#     cluster_1 = factor(cluster_1, levels = rev(unique(cluster_1))),
#     cluster_2 = factor(cluster_2, levels = unique(cluster_2))
#   ) %>%
#   ggplot(aes(cluster_2, cluster_1, fill = probability)) +
#   geom_tile(color = 'white') +
#   geom_text(aes(label = round(probability, digits = 2)), size = 2.5) +
#   scale_x_discrete(name = 'Cluster', position = 'top') +
#   scale_y_discrete(name = 'Cluster') +
#   scale_fill_gradient(
#     name = 'Probability', low = 'white', high = '#c0392b', na.value = '#bdc3c7',
#     limits = c(0,1),
#     guide = guide_colorbar(
#       frame.colour = 'black', ticks.colour = 'black', title.position = 'left',
#       title.theme = element_text(hjust = 1, angle = 90),
#       barwidth = 0.75, barheight = 10
#     )
#   ) +
#   coord_fixed() +
#   theme_bw() +
#   theme(
#     legend.position = 'right',
#     panel.grid.major = element_blank()
#   )
# p
```

##### Silouhette score

Let's look at the silouhette score to assess the quality of our clusters.

::: callout-note
The silhouette score measures how similar a cell is to its own cluster compared to other clusters, with values ranging from -1 to 1. A high silhouette score indicates that cells are well-matched to their own cluster and poorly matched to neighboring clusters, suggesting that the clusters are well-defined and distinct. Conversely, a low silhouette score may indicate that cells are not well-matched to their own cluster or that there is significant overlap between clusters, suggesting that the clustering solution may not be optimal. By calculating the silhouette score for our CD8 T cell clusters, we can gain insights into the quality and validity of our clustering results and identify potential issues that may need to be addressed in downstream analyses.
:::

::: callout-tip
Maybe later, try bluster::approxSilhouette()
:::

```{r CD8 Silhouette score}
library(cluster)

emb <- Embeddings(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, "harmony")[, 1:13]
clust <- as.integer(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters)

sil <- silhouette(clust, dist(emb))

sil_df <- data.frame(
  sil = sil[, "sil_width"],
  cluster = factor(clust)
)

all_silouhette <- ggplot(sil_df, aes(x = sil, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 100, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Silhouette width", y = "Cell count", fill = "Cluster") + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal)
all_silouhette

dir.create(
  file.path(DIR_RESULT,
            "CD8_Tcells",
            "Clustering",
            "Silhouette"),
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE)

ggsave(
  plot = all_silouhette,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Silhouette",
    "Silhouette_width_histogram.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)

```

```{r CD8 Silhouette violin plot}
violin_silouhette <- ggplot(sil_df, aes(x = cluster, y = sil, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_violin(trim = FALSE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, fill = "white") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Cluster", y = "Silhouette width") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal) +
  NoLegend()
violin_silouhette

violin_silouhette <- ggsave(
  plot = violin_silouhette,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Silhouette",
    "Silhouette_width_violin.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
violin_silouhette
```

```{r CD8 Silhouette mean per cluster}
mean_sil <- sil_df %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(mean_sil = mean(sil))

mean_silouhette <- ggplot(mean_sil, aes(x = cluster, y = mean_sil, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Cluster", y = "Mean silhouette width") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal) +
  NoLegend()
mean_silouhette

ggsave(
  plot = mean_silouhette,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Silhouette",
    "Silhouette_mean_width_per_cluster.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

```{r CD8 Classic Silhouette plot}
classic_silouhette <- plot(sil, border = NA)
classic_silouhette

ggsave(
  plot = classic_silouhette,
  filename = file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Clustering",
    "Silhouette",
    "Silhouette_classic_plot.png"),
  width = 12,
  height = 6)
```

```{r CD8 Silhouette Run}
# embeddings PCA
emb <- Embeddings(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, "harmony")[, 1:13]

# clusters
clust <- as.numeric(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters)

# silhouette
sil <- silhouette(clust, dist(emb))

# moyenne globale
mean(sil[, "sil_width"])

sil_df <- data.frame(
  cluster = clust,
  sil_width = sil[, "sil_width"]
)


```

```{r CD8 Silhouette table}
sil_df %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(mean_silhouette = mean(sil_width))


sil_summary <- sil_df %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_sil = mean(sil_width),
    median_sil = median(sil_width)
  ) %>%
  arrange(mean_sil)

sil_summary
```

```{r CD8 Silhouette Viz}
ggplot(sil_df, aes(x = sil_width, y = reorder(cluster, sil_width))) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  labs(
    x = "Silhouette width",
    y = "Cluster",
    title = "Silhouette plot (cell-level)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# ggplot(sil_df, aes(x = cluster, y = sil_width)) +
#   geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 0.3) +
#   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
#   theme_minimal() +
#   labs(
#     y = "Silhouette width",
#     x = "Cluster",
#     title = "Silhouette score per cluster"
#   )

# seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$silhouette <- sil[, "sil_width"]
# 
# FeaturePlot(
#   seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
#   features = "silhouette",
#   cols = c("blue", "white", "red"),
#   order = TRUE
# )

```

The quality of clusters found are not really good...

==\> Clusters found are not drive by technical biases, but they are not really good either, as the silhouette scores are quite low, with a mean silhouette width of around 0.1, which suggests that the clusters are not well-defined and that there may be significant overlap between clusters. This could indicate that the clustering solution may not be optimal and that further investigation is needed to identify potential issues with the clustering results and to improve the quality of the clusters identified in our CD8 T cell dataset.

#### Remove bad clusters {.tabset}

According to me, I don't see any cluster to remove based on QC metrics, cell cycle phase distribution, and patient distribution. All clusters seem to have a reasonable number of cells, a mix of cell cycle phases. Therefore, I will keep all clusters for downstream analyses. But cells are attributed to clusters with a low confidence, so I will keep in mind that these clusters may not be well-defined and that there may be significant overlap between clusters, which could impact the interpretation of downstream analyses.

```{r CD8 Remove bad clusters}
# Remove clusters 
# seu_CD8_QC_Moderate <- subset(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
#               subset = !(seurat_clusters %in% c("8", "9","10")))
# 
# seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters <- factor(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters)
```

```{r CD8 UMAP without bad clusters}
# DimPlot(seu, reduction = "umap", group.by = "seurat_clusters") + ggtitle("seurat clusters after removing bad clusters")
# 
# ggsave("03_result/Clustering/UMAP_seurat_clusters_after_removing_bad_clusters.png")
# 
# DimPlot(seu, reduction = "umap", group.by = "seurat_clusters") + ggtitle("seurat clusters after removing bad clusters") +
#   facet_wrap(~seurat_clusters, ncol = (length(unique(seu$seurat_clusters))/2) )
# 
# ggsave("03_result/Clustering/UMAP_seurat_clusters_after_removing_bad_clusters_facet.png")
# 
# ### !!!!!!! ADD A FACET VERSION !!!!!!! ###
# ### !!!!! and save !!!!! ###
```

<br>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<br>

# Annotation {.tabset}

Let's try to annotate our clusters based on the expression of known marker genes for CD8 T cell subpopulations. We can use the FeaturePlot function to visualize the expression of these marker genes on the UMAP plot and see if they are enriched in specific clusters. We can also look at the average expression of these marker genes in each cluster to further characterize their identity and function.

::::::::::: panel-tabset
## FeaturePlot prot (CITE-seq) + RNA (scRNAseq) {.tabset}

```{r CD8 FeaturePlot ADT}
dict <- list(
  "CD16-TotalSeqB" = "FCGR3A",
  "CD335-NKp46-TotalSeqB" = "NCR1",
  "CD197-CCR7-TotalSeqB" = "CCR7",
  "CD4-TotalSeqB" = "CD4",
  "CD8a-TotalSeqB" = "CD8A",
  "CD45RA-TotalSeqB" = "PTPRC",
  "CD62L-TotalSeqB" = "SELL",
  "CD127-IL-7Ra-TotalSeqB" = "IL7R",
  "CD25-TotalSeqB" = "IL2RA",
  "TIGIT-VSTM3-TotalSeqB" = "TIGIT",
  "CD226-DNAM-1-TotalSeqB" = "CD226",
  "CD38-TotalSeqB" = "CD38"
)

dir.create(
  file.path(DIR_RESULT,
            "CD8_Tcells",
            "FeaturePlot",
            "Prot_RNA_Clusters_FeaturePlot"),
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE)

for (prot in names(dict)) {
  gene <- dict[[prot]]
  
  # ADT plot
  DefaultAssay(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "ADT"
  p1 <- FeaturePlot(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, features = prot, reduction = "umap") + ggtitle(paste0(prot, " (ADT)"))
  
  # RNA plot
  DefaultAssay(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "RNA"
  # Vérifie que le gène existe dans RNA
  if (gene %in% rownames(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate[["RNA"]])) {
    p2 <- FeaturePlot(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, features = gene, reduction = "umap") + ggtitle(paste0(gene, " (RNA)"))
  } else {
    p2 <- ggplot() + 
      ggtitle(paste0(gene, " (RNA) - Not found")) + 
      theme_void()
  }
  
  p3 <- DimPlot(
    seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
    reduction = "umap", 
    group.by = "seurat_clusters") + 
    ggtitle("seurat_clusters") +
    scale_color_manual(values = pal)
  
  # Combine plots
  p <- (p1 | p2) / p3
  print(p)

  # Sauvegarde
  ggsave(
    filename = file.path(
      DIR_RESULT,
      "CD8_Tcells",
      "FeaturePlot",
      "Prot_RNA_Clusters_FeaturePlot",
      paste0("Prot_RNA_Clusters_FeaturePlot_", prot, "_", gene, "_featureplot.png")),
    plot = p,
    width = 12,
    height = 14)
}
```

## Clusters markers {.tabset}

Compute top10 markers for each cluster to see if they correspond to known CD8 T cell subpopulations and to further characterize their identity and function.

### Run {.tabset}

We use Find all markers function to find differentially expressed genes between clusters, and we filter the results to keep only significant markers with an adjusted p-value \< 0.05 and a log fold change \> 0.5n min percentage of cells expressing the gene in either cluster of at least 25%.

```{r CD8 FindAllMarkers}
Idents(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate) <- "seurat_clusters"


markers <- FindAllMarkers(
  seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
  test.use = "wilcox",
  assay = "SCT",
  slot = "data",
  only.pos = FALSE, 
  min.pct = 0.25,
  # latent.vars = c("nCount_RNA", "emulsion", "percent.mt", "Phase"),
  verbose = TRUE,
  random.seed = 42)

markers_filt <- markers %>%
  filter(
    p_val_adj < 0.05,
    avg_log2FC > 0.5,
    !grepl("^MT-|^RPL|^RPS", gene)
  )
```

#### Pull Top 10 {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Top10 markers}
genes.scaled <- rownames(seu_CD8_QC_Moderate[["RNA"]]@scale.data)

top10 <- markers_filt %>%
  filter(gene %in% genes.scaled) %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  slice_max(avg_log2FC, n = 10)

top10 %>% arrange(cluster, desc(avg_log2FC))

# If p_val and p_val_adj are 0.0000000000000, transform them to 1e-300 for better visualization
top10 <- top10 %>%
  mutate(
    p_val = formatC(p_val, format = "e", digits = 3),
    p_val_adj = formatC(p_val_adj, format = "e", digits = 3)
  )
```

### Visualize

:::: panel-tabset
#### Feature {.tabset}

```{r CD8 FeaturePlot Top10}
dir.create(
  file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "FeaturePlot",
    "Top10_Markers_Per_Cluster"),
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE)

for (i in unique(top10$cluster)) {
  
  dir.create(
    file.path(
      DIR_RESULT,
      "CD8_Tcells",
      "FeaturePlot",
      "Top10_Markers_Per_Cluster",
      paste0("Cluster_", i)),
    recursive = TRUE,
    showWarnings = FALSE
  )

  genes_cluster <- top10 %>%
    filter(cluster == i) %>%
    pull(gene)

  p_list <- lapply(genes_cluster, function(gene) {
    FeaturePlot(
      seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
      features = gene, 
      reduction = "umap") +
      # scale_color_gradient(low = "lightgrey", high = "red") +
      ggtitle(paste0("Cluster ", i, ": ", gene))
  })

  p_combined <- wrap_plots(p_list, ncol = 5)
  # print(p_combined)
  
  ggsave(
    filename = file.path(
      DIR_RESULT,
      "CD8_Tcells",
      "FeaturePlot",
      "Top10_Markers_Per_Cluster",
      paste0("Cluster_", i),
      paste0("Cluster_", i, "_Top10_Markers_featureplot.png")),
    plot = p_combined,
    width = 20,
    height = 12)
  
  lapply(genes_cluster, function(gene) {
    
    dir.create(
      file.path(
        DIR_RESULT,
        "CD8_Tcells",
        "FeaturePlot",
        "Top10_Markers_Per_Cluster",
        paste0("Cluster_", i),
        paste0("Gene_", gene)),
      recursive = TRUE,
      showWarnings = FALSE)
      
    violin <- VlnPlot(
      seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
      cols = pal,
      features = gene, 
      group.by = "seurat_clusters", 
      pt.size = 0) +
      ggtitle(paste0("Cluster ", i, ": ", gene))
    # print(violin)
      
    ggsave(
      plot = violin,
      filename = file.path(
        DIR_RESULT,
        "CD8_Tcells",
        "FeaturePlot",
        "Top10_Markers_Per_Cluster",
        paste0("Cluster_", i),
        paste0("Gene_", gene),
        paste0("Cluster_", i, "_", gene, "_VlnPlot.png")
      ),
      width = 6,
      height = 4)
      
    feature <- FeaturePlot(
      seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
      features = gene, 
      reduction = "umap") +
      ggtitle(paste0("Cluster ", i, ": ", gene))
    # print(feature)
    
    ggsave(
      filename = file.path(
        DIR_RESULT,
        "CD8_Tcells",
        "FeaturePlot",
        "Top10_Markers_Per_Cluster",
        paste0("Cluster_", i),
        paste0("Gene_", gene),
        paste0("Cluster_", i, "_", gene, "_FeaturePlot.png")
      ),
      plot = feature,
      width = 6,
      height = 5)
      
    p_cluster <- DimPlot(
      seu_CD8_QC_Moderate, 
      reduction = "umap", 
      group.by = "seurat_clusters") +
      scale_color_manual(values = pal)
    # print(p_cluster)
      
    p_violin_feature_cluster <- (violin | feature) / p_cluster
    print(p_violin_feature_cluster)
      
    ggsave(
      filename = file.path(
        DIR_RESULT,
        "CD8_Tcells",
        "FeaturePlot",
        "Top10_Markers_Per_Cluster",
        paste0("Cluster_", i),
        paste0("Gene_", gene),
        paste0("Cluster_", i, "_", gene, "_Vln_Feature_Cluster.png")
      ),
      plot = p_violin_feature_cluster,
      width = 12,
      height = 10)
  })
}
```

#### Heatmap {.tabset}

::: callout-note
Heatmap not so much interesting here
:::

```{r CD8 Heatmap Top10}
# genes <- unique(top10$gene)
# 
# # Extract scaled data for the selected genes
# mat <- GetAssayData(
#   seu_CD8_QC_Moderate,
#   assay = "SCT",
#   layer = "scale.data"
# )[genes, ]
# 
# # Order cells by cluster
# clusters <- seu_CD8_QC_Moderate$seurat_clusters
# ord <- order(clusters)
# 
# mat <- mat[, ord]
# clusters <- clusters[ord]
# 
# # Create annotation for clusters
# cluster_ha <- HeatmapAnnotation(
#   Cluster = clusters,
#   col = list(
#     Cluster = structure(
#       Seurat::DiscretePalette(length(unique(clusters)), "polychrome"),
#       names = levels(clusters)
#     )
#   )
# )
# # Create heatmap
# heatmap <- Heatmap(
#   mat,
#   name = "Expression",
#   top_annotation = cluster_ha,
#   show_column_names = FALSE,
#   show_row_names = TRUE,
#   cluster_columns = FALSE,
#   cluster_rows = TRUE,
#   row_names_gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 8),
#   column_title = "Top 10 markers per cluster"
#   )
# heatmap
# 
# 
# # Save heatmap
# dir.create(
#   file.path(
#     DIR_RESULT,
#     "CD8_Tcells",
#     "Heatmap"),
#   recursive = TRUE,
#   showWarnings = FALSE)
# 
# pdf(
#   file = file.path(
#     DIR_RESULT,
#     "CD8_Tcells",
#     "Heatmap",
#     "Top10_Markers_Per_Cluster_Heatmap.pdf"
#   ),
#   width = 12,
#   height = 10
# )
# 
# draw(heatmap)
# 
# dev.off()
# 
# 
# png(
#   filename = file.path(
#     DIR_RESULT,
#     "CD8_Tcells",
#     "Heatmap",
#     "Top10_Markers_Per_Cluster_Heatmap.png"
#   ),
#   width = 2000,
#   height = 1600,
#   res = 300
# )
# 
# draw(heatmap)
# 
# dev.off()

```

#### Volcano {.tabset}

```{r CD8 Volcano plot Top10}
# Volcano plot for each cluster
dir.create(
  file.path(
    DIR_RESULT,
    "CD8_Tcells",
    "Annotation",
    "Volcano_Plots"),
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE)


for (i in unique(top10$cluster)) {

  df <- markers %>%
    filter(cluster == i) %>%
    mutate(
      Status = case_when(
        p_val_adj < 0.05 & avg_log2FC >  0.5 ~ "Up",
        p_val_adj < 0.05 & avg_log2FC < -0.5 ~ "Down",
        TRUE ~ "NS"
      )
    )

  # Top genes à annoter
  top_up <- df %>%
  filter(Status == "Up") %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_log2FC)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

  top_down <- df %>%
    filter(Status == "Down") %>%
    arrange(avg_log2FC) %>%   # plus négatif en premier
    slice_head(n = 10)

  top_genes <- bind_rows(top_up, top_down)

  p <- ggplot(df, aes(avg_log2FC, -log10(p_val_adj))) +
    geom_point(aes(color = Status), size = 1, alpha = 0.7) +

    geom_vline(xintercept = c(-0.5, 0.5), linetype = "dashed", color = "black") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed", color = "black") +

    geom_text_repel(
      data = top_genes,
      aes(label = gene),
      size = 3,
      max.overlaps = Inf
    ) +

    scale_color_manual(
      values = c(
        "Up"   = "#D62728",
        "Down" = "#1F77B4",
        "NS"   = "grey70"
      )
    ) +

    labs(
```
